title: Carved sycamore egg cup
subtitle: A whittled and hook-carved egg cup in seasoned sycamore
excerpt: An egg cup combines two of the core knife skills into one small object: the outside is whittled to a goblet profile with a sloyd knife, and the inside cup is hollowed with a hook knife. Sycamore is the traditional British egg cup wood because it is food-safe, easy to carve, and pale enough to show whether the cup is clean. This intermediate project covers cutting the outside taper, hollowing the cup with a hook knife, and applying a raw linseed oil finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (1869) for sycamore as the traditional British egg cup wood. Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for the carved wooden utensil tradition.

content.text: An egg cup is the classic pairing exercise in Scandinavian carving. The outside teaches the sloyd-knife taper cut. The inside teaches the
hook knife
hollow. A
seasoned
blank is carved dry, so the cup will not warp after the finish goes on. Raw linseed oil, brushed on after carving, makes the sycamore food-safe.
What you need: wood

A seasoned sycamore blank, 8 cm long, 5 cm by 5 cm in section, with the grain running along the length. The blank must be fully dry; a slightly damp blank will develop a small crack in the cup interior as it dries around the hollow.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife for the outside profile. A hook knife (small) for the cup hollow. A leather strop throughout; sycamore is a dense hardwood and dulls a hook knife faster than softwood. Sandpaper at 180 and 240. Raw linseed oil.
Safety
The hook knife is pulled toward the body; keep the thumb of the holding hand behind the blade's path, not in front of it.
Push cuts
on the outside taper are directed away from the body with the workpiece held against a bench or supported on the knee.
The work
Step 1: Mark the profile

Mark the profile on the blank. The cup portion (top 4 cm): a cylinder 4 cm in diameter at the rim, flaring slightly at the base. The stem (middle 2 cm): a narrow waist approximately 22 mm in diameter. The base (bottom 2 cm): a widening foot approximately 40 mm in diameter for stability. Draw the outline on all four sides with a pencil.
Step 2: Rough the outside profile

With the sloyd knife, remove the square corners of the blank to produce an octagonal section. Use push cuts working from the rim toward the stem and from the base toward the stem; cutting into the waist from either end keeps the grain running with the blade rather than tearing. Bring the blank to a rough round section, establishing the stem waist and the foot flare. At this stage the hollow is not yet started.
Step 3: Hollow the cup

Mark the hollow: a circle 34 mm in diameter centred on the top face of the blank. Begin the hollow with the sloyd knife, boring a starting nick at the centre. Switch to the hook knife. Pull the hook knife from the edge of the circle toward the centre, rotating the blank between passes to work around the full circumference. The hook knife works best in sycamore when pulling across the grain at about 45 degrees. Hollow to 30 mm depth, leaving 5 mm wall thickness. Strop the hook knife every ten passes.
Step 4: Refine the outside and sand

Return to the sloyd knife to refine the outside profile after the hollow is complete: the cup portion is now more easily held in the hand, and the remaining facets on the outside can be reduced. Sand the outside with 180 then 240 grit. Sand the inside of the hollow with a folded piece of 180 grit. Do not sand into the rim edge from the outside; keep a clean, sharp rim.
Finishing

Apply raw linseed oil inside and outside the cup: two coats, one per day, wiping off the excess after 30 minutes each time. Raw linseed oil is food-safe once fully set (allow 3 to 7 days after the final coat before food contact).
Variations
A longer stem (4 cm instead of 2 cm) gives an Edwardian look. The waist narrows to 18 mm. Carve the stem with slow cuts from each side rather than a single long pass.
Cherry darkens rapidly with use and oil, giving an egg cup that improves in colour over months of use; a useful first gift piece.
Care
Hand-wash and dry immediately. Re-oil once every few months when the surface looks dry. Do not boil or dishwash.

attrs.intro: Problems carving a sycamore egg cup.

items.fix: Strop the hook knife and reduce the cut depth. In sycamore, take thinner shavings than in lime or softwood.
items.cause: The edge is dull. Sycamore dulls a hook knife quickly.
items.symptom: The hook knife chatters rather than cutting

items.fix: Cut the hollow before narrowing the stem to its final dimension; carve the stem to final size last, when the blank can be held more gently.
items.cause: The stem was carved too thin before the cup hollow was cut, and the pressure of holding the blank cracked the narrow waist.

attrs.heading: Common problems
